text: Nominal 1x lumber is actually 3/4 inch thick. A 1x12 is 3/4 x 11-1/4 inch, a 1x4 is 3/4 x 3-1/2 inch, a 1x3 is 3/4 x 2-1/2 inch, a 1x2 is 3/4 x 1-1/2 inch. Always design cut lists from the real dressed dimensions, not the nominal label.
Nominal 2x lumber is 1-1/2 inch thick. A 2x4 is 1-1/2 x 3-1/2 inch, a 2x2 is 1-1/2 x 1-1/2 inch. 2x stock is good for table legs and load paths; 1x stock is good for aprons, rails, and shelves.

Common plywood thicknesses sold in the US are 1/4, 1/2, and 3/4 inch (actual sizes run slightly under). 3/4 inch hardwood plywood (oak, birch, maple) is the standard choice for tabletops and shelves because it stays flat and holds screws in the face but not the edge.

Pre-drill a pilot hole before driving wood screws near board ends to prevent splitting. For 1-1/4 inch #8 wood screws in pine, use about a 7/64 inch pilot. Screws into end grain hold poorly; prefer face-grain joints, pocket holes, or glue blocks.

Pick screw length so it never pokes through the visible face. For 3/4 inch tabletops fastened from underneath through a 3/4 inch apron, use screws around 1-1/4 inch and stop short of the top surface. Use figure-8 fasteners or slotted clips if you want a tabletop to expand and contract.

Beginner-safe joinery for low-risk furniture: butt joints with glue and screws, pocket-hole joints, dowels, and metal corner brackets. Avoid mortise-and-tenon or dovetails for an MVP. Always glue plus mechanically fasten; glue alone fails in tension.

Standard furniture heights: dining table about 28-30 inch, desk about 29-30 inch, coffee table about 16-18 inch, side/nightstand table about 24-26 inch, kitchen counter about 36 inch. Round dining tables for 4-6 people are usually 42-54 inch in diameter.

To avoid sagging, keep an unsupported 3/4 inch shelf span under about 30 inch for books. Add a back panel or a center support for longer spans. Deeper shelves (11-12 inch) suit books; 6-8 inch suits decor.

Water-based polyurethane dries fast, stays clear, and cleans up with water; apply 2-3 thin coats sanding lightly between coats with 220 grit. Oil-based poly is more durable but ambers and needs ventilation. Always finish in a ventilated space.

Typical sanding progression for furniture: 80-120 grit to flatten and remove mill marks, 150-180 grit to refine, 220 grit before finishing. Sand with the grain. Ease (round over) sharp edges so they are safe and take finish evenly.

Low-risk DIY furniture excludes electrical wiring, gas, plumbing, ceiling-mounted or wall-anchored load-bearing structures, and seating or climbing furniture that carries dynamic human loads. If a reference shows those, scope the plan down to a non-structural, non-electrical simplified version and add a warning.

Tall furniture (bookshelves, tall nightstands) can tip. Recommend anti-tip wall straps for anything taller than it is deep, keep heavy items low, and widen the base. Note this as a safety check rather than engineering it in the MVP.

Home Depot and Lowe's both stock dimensional pine, hardwood boards (poplar, oak), project panels, plywood, wood screws, pocket-hole jigs, wood glue, sandpaper, and water-based polyurethane. Generate plain search queries like 'select pine board 1x12' or '3/4 inch oak plywood' rather than promising exact live stock.

Rough 2025 US hardware-store prices: 1x12x8 pine ~$18, 1x3x8 pine ~$9, 2x4x8 ~$6-9, 3/4 inch 2x4 ft oak plywood panel ~$40-70, 1 lb box wood screws ~$8, wood glue ~$6, quart water-based poly ~$22. Keep the itemized material total within the stated low/high estimate.

Cut a round tabletop by marking a circle with a pivoting jig (a screw, string, and pencil or a router trammel) and cutting just outside the line with a jigsaw, then sand to the line. For large tops, cut two semicircles and join them with a center seam batten and alignment connectors.

Commercial pedestal tables often use a central hub plate with four triangular wedge tabs. Each foot arm has a dado/notch that slides horizontally onto a wedge until a shoulder seats against the hub flange. Lock each wedge to the hub with bolts or screws. Do not rely on friction-only butt joints for radial arms.

DIY pedestal columns: (1) 3 inch steel pipe or PVC core wrapped with 1/4 inch MDF staves, glued and sanded, then painted; or (2) laminated 3/4 inch MDF rings around a core. Flare the top with a turned MDF collar or steel disc for the tabletop mount.

Factory-painted furniture colours (red, blue, etc.) cannot be matched with bare lumber. Use multi-surface primer, then 2 thin coats of semi-gloss interior latex tinted to the reference (bring a photo or hex code to the paint counter). Sample pots first. Alternative: powder-coat steel pipe at an auto body shop.

Pedestal cross bases wobble on uneven floors. Thread 1/4-20 inserts into the underside of each foot arm and use adjustable leveling feet (thumb-screw style). Install feet before final paint so threads stay clean.

When a source assembly booklet is provided, preserve its step order, hardware counts, part numbers, arrows, insets, and page-level evidence. Do not invent extra cutting, sanding, or finishing steps in source-manual replica mode.

IKEA-style manuals treat hardware IDs as part of the task. Preserve visible SKUs such as 195312 leveling foot, 10074126 screw, 115461 screw, and 100092 Allen key. Link the inventory icon to each later use.
Small fastener operations should be shown with a circular zoom inset, a leader line to the connection point, an explicit count label, and a curved rotation arrow when threading or tightening.

Use a small visual operation vocabulary for assembly pages: insert_horizontal, drop_vertical, thread_rotate, tighten_with_tool, adjust_level, and check_level. Each step should have one primary operation.
